tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of insomnia as the primary diagnosis based on history and exclusion of key secondary causes/red flags.

nl_assertions: Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, non-restorative sleep, and associated daytime impairment.
Clarifies duration and pattern (acute vs chronic), frequency, and precipitating/maintaining factors (stress, schedule changes, naps, irregular sleep-wake timing).

Screens for secondary causes and common mimics: depression/anxiety, bipolar/mania symptoms, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, cannabis), medication contributors (stimulants, SSRIs/SNRIs, corticosteroids, decongestants), pain, GERD, nocturia.

Screens for sleep disorders requiring different management: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements (urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening), circadian rhythm disorder (shift work, delayed sleep phase).
Assesses safety risks and urgency: suicidal ideation, severe functional impairment, high-risk occupations (driving, machinery), and severe daytime sleepiness; advises against driving when drowsy.

nl_assertions: States Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the leading diagnosis (or strongly suspected diagnosis) consistent with a hereditary peripheral neuropathy presentation.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies central diabetes insipidus to guide appropriate ADH-replacement therapy and prevent mismanagement (e.g., inappropriate fluid restriction or missed pituitary pathology).
Initiates evidence-based first-line therapy (desmopressin) to reduce polyuria/polydipsia and prevent dehydration/hypernatremia while emphasizing safe use to avoid hyponatremia.

required_args.reason: Confirm etiology of central diabetes insipidus, optimize desmopressin regimen, and evaluate pituitary/hypothalamic cause

tool_call_sequence.description: Ensures specialist evaluation for underlying pituitary/hypothalamic disease and long-term management (dose titration, monitoring, imaging/etiology workup).

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "central diabetes insipidus" and "ADH", describing inadequate ADH production/release leading to inability to concentrate urine and resulting polyuria/polydipsia.
Explains treatment using the terms "central diabetes insipidus" and "desmopressin", including that desmopressin replaces ADH effect and reduces urine output and thirst.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: contraindication_check and drug_interaction_check (critical), and allergy_check (non-critical); does not prescribe until critical checks are addressed.

Counsels on desmopressin safety: risk of water intoxication/hyponatremia, advises appropriate fluid intake (avoid excessive free water), and provides clear red-flag symptoms (headache, nausea/vomiting, confusion, seizures) requiring urgent evaluation.

Demonstrates appropriate monitoring plan in narrative (even if not using a WRITE tool): follow-up for symptom response and serum sodium/osmolality after initiation or dose changes, and reassessment if symptoms persist or hyponatremia symptoms occur.

Recognizes the need to assess/communicate possible underlying causes (pituitary surgery/trauma, tumors, infiltrative/autoimmune disease) and justifies endocrinology referral for etiology workup and long-term management.

Avoids unsafe or incorrect therapies for central diabetes insipidus (e.g., recommending fluid restriction as primary therapy, using loop diuretics as first-line, or treating as nephrogenic DI without rationale).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose central diabetes insipidus, start desmopressin with safety checks and counseling, and refer to endocrinology for etiology evaluation and regimen optimization.
must_collect: history of head trauma or neurosurgery
serum sodium/urine osmolality results if available
steps: Synthesize history and available labs to support central diabetes insipidus (hypotonic polyuria with inappropriately dilute urine and/or response to desmopressin, when available).
Communicate diagnosis: central diabetes insipidus due to deficient ADH leading to excessive dilute urine and thirst.
Complete contraindication_check and drug_interaction_check (and allergy_check if available) before prescribing.
Prescribe desmopressin with a conservative starting approach and clear patient instructions, including fluid guidance and hyponatremia warning signs.
Arrange endocrinology referral to confirm diagnosis/etiology and plan pituitary evaluation and long-term monitoring.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a broader differential discussion before committing, then proceeds with central DI diagnosis, desmopressin, and endocrine referral.
steps: Discuss differential for polyuria/polydipsia (primary polydipsia, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, nephrogenic DI, diuretic use) and explain why central DI is most consistent with the data provided.
Record diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus.
Perform contraindication and interaction checks (and allergy check if available).
Prescribe desmopressin and counsel on hyponatremia risk and fluid intake.
Refer to endocrinology for etiology workup and management.

acceptable_variants.description: Emphasizes safety monitoring and follow-up logistics while still meeting L3 outcome actions.
steps: Record diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus with brief explanation referencing ADH deficiency.
Before prescribing, explicitly address contraindications (e.g., baseline hyponatremia, significant renal impairment) and medication interactions that increase hyponatremia risk.
Prescribe desmopressin with explicit plan for sodium monitoring after initiation/dose change and instructions to hold dose and seek care if hyponatremia symptoms occur.
Refer to endocrinology for confirmation and pituitary evaluation.
